reasoning: The ACLED dataset shows a historical baseline of ~1 'Violence against civilians' event per 360 days in Belgium, which is a stable and highly advanced democracy. A positive resolution for any of the dates would require an 11-fold increase in the monthly rate over the historical average (i.e., at least 11 events of organized armed violence against civilians occurring within a single 30-day window). Sustaining or even reaching such a high level of violence (characteristic of an active civil conflict or unprecedented sustained terrorist campaign) is extremely unlikely in Belgium. Additionally, if ACLED were to change its coding methodology (e.g., reclassifying gang activity), they historically backcode their dataset; since the baseline is recalculated from the updated dataset at resolution time, artificial threshold breaks from methodological updates are effectively buffered. Given this enormous structural hurdle and lack of any current indicators of escalating structured violence, the probability remains very near the lower bound allowed by the forecasting limits (0.05). I've scaled the probabilities up very slightly for later dates to account for the longer time horizon and general uncertainty, although the true underlying base-rate probability is well below 1%.
